summary: DHH celebrates Meta's announcement to end third-party fact-checking and relax content moderation on its platforms, framing it as the end of a social media censorship era. He argues that the pandemic period saw governments directly instruct platforms to suppress legitimate speech under the guise of fighting 'misinformation' and 'hate speech.' He credits Elon Musk's acquisition of Twitter as the pivotal moment that broke the censorship consensus, and gives Trump's election win credit for accelerating the shift. He contrasts America's move toward free expression with Europe and the UK's continued embrace of speech restrictions, arguing the world needs America's First Amendment principles.
key_points: Meta's pivot to community notes and relaxed moderation across Facebook, Instagram, and Threads affects three billion users and represents a seismic shift in online speech policy

The pandemic-era censorship regime used 'hate speech' and 'misinformation' as rhetorical fig leaves that quickly lost objective meaning and became tools for suppressing politically inconvenient speech
Musk's acquisition and transformation of Twitter into X was the pivotal moment that broke the global censorship consensus and enabled Meta's follow-on pivot
A growing chasm exists between America's recovery from speech restrictions and Europe/UK's doubling down on censorship, with UK police threatening arrests over social media posts
Free speech protections must extend to speech you disagree with — a principle illustrated by the ACLU defending Nazis' right to march in Skokie
money_quotes: Terms that almost immediately lost all objective content, and turned into mere descriptors of 'speech we don't like'.
This is why the world needs America. From the UK to the EU to Brazil, China, Russia, and Iran, political censorship is very popular.
His acquisition of Twitter and its transformation into X was the pivotal moment for both American and global free speech.
It's hard to divorce principles from people! We're all liable to mix and confuse the two.
You don't need to love every champion of your principles to quietly appreciate their contributions.
In order for you to voice your dissent on Trump or Musk or whatever, you need the protection of the first amendment to cover those who want to dissent in the opposite direction too.
It's entire purpose is to protect speech that's unpopular with the rulers of the moment.
I will cheer for Zuckerberg without reservation when he works in their service.
key_insight: The collapse of the pandemic-era censorship consensus — triggered by Musk's Twitter acquisition and cemented by Meta's pivot — marks a historic victory for free expression, but the principle only endures if defended for speech you oppose.
